data.title: SOBR Safe Agrees to Merge With Clean World Ventures
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

SOBR Safe agreed to merge its eponymous alcohol monitoring and detection technology business with Clean World Ventures, a zero-carbon energy technology company.

Shares of SOBR Safe surged 30% to 71 cents a share in after-hours trading. Ahead of the market close, the stock closed up 2.1% at 55 cents a share, and is down 74% year to date.

The combined business would operate as Clean World Ventures, and CWV would have a roughly 98% stake, upon the close of the deal. CWV will also determine the executive leadership and board members of the combined company.

"The intent is for the SOBR Safe and CWV businesses to continue to operate independently upon consummation of the Proposed Transaction and SOBR Safe plans to continue evaluating opportunities to monetize the value of its alcohol monitoring and detection technology business," the companies said Thursday.

The combined company would focus on energy technology, with a goal of meeting clean energy demands of artificial-intelligence data center builders.

"The Proposed Transaction with SOBR Safe represents a compelling opportunity to strategically align capital, innovation, and shared purpose," CWV Chief Executive Roy DiBenerdini said. "We believe this combination positions us to expand our market reach, support a growing base of hyperscale and global enterprise customers, and accelerate the transformation of the global energy industry through our distributed clean energy appliances."

The full financial terms of the deal weren't disclosed.

The transaction is expected to close in the third quarter, subject to certain conditions, including around $5.5 million in pre-closed third-party financing committed to SOBR Safe. The SOBR Safe operating company will use $2 million of that financing upon closing, the companies said.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Robert Half Announces Quarterly Dividend
data.description:
MENLO PARK, Calif., April 30, 2026 /PRNewswire/ — Robert Half Inc. today announced that its board of directors declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.59 per share on the company's common stock. The dividend is payable on June 15, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business on May 22, 2026.

About Robert Half

Robert Half is the world's first and largest specialized talent solutions and business consulting firm, connecting highly skilled job seekers with rewarding opportunities at great companies. We offer contract talent and permanent placement solutions in the fields of finance and accounting, technology, marketing and creative, legal, and administrative and customer support, and we also provide executive search services. Robert Half is the parent company of Protiviti, a global consulting firm that delivers internal audit, risk, business and technology consulting solutions. In the past 12 months, Robert Half has been recognized as one of America's Most Innovative Companies by Fortune and, with Protiviti, has been named as a Fortune(R) Most Admired Company(TM) and one of the 100 Best Companies to Work For(R) . Explore talent solutions, research and insights at roberthalf.com.

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/robert-half-announces-quarterly-dividend-302759541.html

SOURCE Robert Half

data.title: Avonova BidCo AB (publ) publishes Annual Report for 2025
data.description:
Avonova Reports 2025: Improved profitability and strengthened financial position

Avonova today publishes its Annual Report for 2025. The Group delivered stable revenue of SEK 1,722.1 million and significantly improved profitability, reflecting stronger margins and continued cost control.

The year was also marked by the successful bond issuance, strengthening the Company’s capital structure and supporting its ongoing strategic development.

The Group closes 2025 with improved profitability and solid liquidity.

The Annual Report is now available at the company website www.avonova.com

About Us

Avonova is Nordic’s leading provider of occupational health services and is the only Nordic occupational health services provider with a fully integrated hybrid digital/physical delivery model.

Avonova offers a comprehensive range of services to promote well-being and reduce absence, among employees of companies and institutions of all sizes and in all industries. It is leading in preventive evidence based occupational health services in highest impact areas, such as presenteeism, vaccination, and drug testing and rehab.

Avonova has its main offices in Larvik, Norway and Stockholm, Sweden and delivers its services digitally and from 166 locations in Norway and Sweden, by about 1 100 employees – mainly physicians, psychiatrists, nurses, and workplace engineers. For more information, visit www.avonova.com.

Attachments

Avonova BidCo Annual Report 2025

data.title: Air Canada Says Demand Remains Robust Amid Energy-Price Surge — Market Talk
data.description: Despite the need to raise fares to address the fuel-price jump, Air Canada says it has yet to see any decline in demand in bookings in the coming quarters. The airline's chief commercial officer, Mark Galardo, tells analysts that the load factor — or the share of available seats filled by paying passengers — is on track in 3Q to exceed prior year levels. Galardo added that North American demand appears "much more resilient" relative to transAtlantic and transPacific markets. (paul.vieira@wsj.com)

data.description:

data.title: First Breach Files to Go Public Via Nasdaq
data.description:
By Christopher Kuo

First Breach filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission to go public via a direct listing on Nasdaq.

The ammunition manufacturer said they registered the resale of up to about 45 million shares and applied to list under the symbol "FBDT," according to the Thursday filing.

The company posted $384,000 in revenue in 2025 and logged a loss of $13.8 million, according to the filing.

Write to Christopher Kuo at chris.kuo@wsj.com

data.title: Ligand Pharmaceuticals Gives Notice of Termination to Viking Therapeutics Over TR-Beta Program
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Ligand Pharmaceuticals delivered notice to Viking Therapeutics that it was terminating the TR-Beta Program.

Ligand said it was terminating the program as a result of its claim that Viking "materially breached its obligation to use Commercially Reasonable Efforts to develop and commercialize the TR-Beta Program under the License Agreement," according to a Thursday filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

The agreement between the companies obligates Viking to grant Ligand a non-exclusive, global royalty-bearing license for products developed under the TR-Beta Program following the termination of the program, Ligand said.

Viking is disputing Ligand's right to terminate the program, according to the filing.

"The Company believes its right to terminate the TR-Beta Program is valid pursuant to the terms of the License Agreement, and intends to vigorously enforce its right to terminate the TR-Beta Program under the License Agreement," Ligand said.

A Viking representative didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Boise Cascade Company Announces Quarterly Dividend of $0.22 Per Share
data.description:
BOISE, Idaho--(BUSINESS WIRE)--April 30, 2026--

Boise Cascade Company's (Boise Cascade or the Company) Board of Directors declared a quarterly dividend of $0.22 per share to holders of its common stock. The dividend will be paid on June 17, 2026 to stockholders of record on June 1, 2026.

Future dividend declarations, including amount per share, record date and payment date, will be made by the board of directors and will depend upon, among other things, legal capital requirements and surplus, the Company's future operations and earnings, general financial condition, material cash requirements, restrictions imposed by our revolving credit facility and the indenture governing our senior notes, applicable laws, and other factors as the board of directors may deem relevant.

About Boise Cascade

Boise Cascade is one of the largest U.S. wholesale distributors of building materials and a leading manufacturer of engineered wood products and plywood in North America. Our integrated model and national distribution footprint position us to deliver outstanding service to our customers across a broad range of industry-leading products, including key structural products that we produce. Headquartered in Boise, Idaho, we operate more than 60 distribution and manufacturing facilities strategically located across the U.S. and Canada. Our work is powered by a dedicated team of over 7,500 people. Learn more at www.bc.com.

Forward-Looking Statements

This press release contains statements concerning future events and expectations, including, without limitation, statements relating to the amount, timing and occurrence of future dividends. These statements constitute forward-looking statements within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. Any statements that express, or involve discussions as to, expectations, beliefs, plans, objectives, assumptions, or future events or performance, often, but not always, through the use of words or phrases such as "anticipates," "believes," "could," "estimates," "expects," "intends," "outlook," "potential," "plans," "predicts," "preliminary," "projects," "targets," "may," "may result," or similar expressions, are not statements of historical facts and may be forward-looking. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future performance, involve estimates, assumptions, risks, and uncertainties, and may differ materially from actual results, performance, or outcomes. Factors that could cause actual results or outcomes to differ materially from those contained in forward-looking statements include: the commodity nature of a portion of our products and their price movements, which are driven largely by general economic conditions, industry capacity and operating rates, industry cycles that affect supply and demand, and net import and export activity; the highly competitive nature of our industry; declines in demand for our products due to competing technologies or materials, as well as changes in building code provisions; and other factors set forth in Boise Cascade's most recent Annual Report on Form 10-K.

It is not possible to predict or identify all risks and uncertainties that might affect the accuracy of our forward-looking statements and, consequently, our descriptions of such risks and uncertainties should not be considered exhaustive. There is no guarantee that any of the events anticipated by these forward-looking statements will occur, and if any of the events do occur, there is no guarantee what effect they will have on the company's business, results of operations, cash flows, financial condition and future prospects. Forward-looking statements speak only as of the date they are made, and, except as required by law, we undertake no obligation to publicly update or revise any forward-looking statements, whether because of new information, future events, or otherwise.

Outlook

  • Badger expects strong end-market growth and customer demand to continue in 2026

Result Drivers

  • STRONG END-MARKET DEMAND - Co said Q1 growth reflected strength in core end markets and customer demand

  • HIGHER FLEET UTILIZATION - Increased utilization and year-over-year fleet growth supported revenue gains

  • ONGOING INVESTMENTS - Co said investments in team and branch network positioned it to benefit from demand

data.title: A Jet to Caracas Becomes the Face of Trump's Venezuela Reset — WSJ
data.description:
By Vera Bergengruen

CARACAS, Venezuela — An American Airlines jet emblazoned with America's 250th-anniversary logo landed here Thursday, completing the first direct commercial flight between the U.S. and Venezuela since 2019 and capping a day of celebration in Miami and Caracas.

The 76-seat American Eagle flight was hailed as the most visible symbol yet of President Trump's rapid normalization of ties with Venezuela, with officials from both countries characterizing the resumption of direct travel as a milestone.

"Direct flights are back," said John Barrett, who recently took up his posting as the top American diplomat in Caracas. "Commerce is flowing. Venezuela's economic engine is ramping up, and we're just getting started."

The festivities began before American Flight 3599 took off from Miami International Airport. At the gate, Venezuelan and U.S. officials, lawmakers, Miami influencers and journalists mingled over cafecitos and arepas as a DJ played reggaeton. Airport workers in vests waved Venezuelan flags as officials and airline executives cut a ribbon.

After the three-hour flight — which at $1,700 round-trip remains out of the reach of most Venezuelans in Miami or their homeland — passengers were greeted by another round of celebrations in Caracas. Speaking at the Simón Bolivar International Airport outside Caracas, American officials said the flight signaled that Venezuela was open for business.

"We are moving at Trump speed to get things done," said Jarrod Agen, a White House energy adviser and director of the National Energy Dominance Council, a body Trump set up to boost energy projects.

The day's events would have been unthinkable four months ago, when the U.S. military bombed Caracas and captured Venezuelan strongman Nicolás Maduro and his wife in an overnight raid. After putting Maduro's vice president, Delcy Rodríguez, in his place, the Trump administration embarked on an unusual arrangement to attract American investors to Venezuela while solidifying ties between the two governments. The effort has assigned higher priority to stability and oil and mining deals than to a democratic transition, leaving Maduro's political machinery intact.

The photo-ops underscored the whiplash of the past four months. Venezuela's Ambassador to the U.S., Félix Plasencia, who previously served as Maduro's foreign minister, stood shoulder-to-shoulder with Miami officials who have long denounced the regime he served. U.S. officials spoke near portraits of Maduro that still hang in the Caracas airport.

"This flight marks the transition from dialogue to action," Plasencia declared in a speech outside the gate in Miami, where a screen showed images of waterfalls and beaches to promote Venezuelan tourism. "We're witnessing one of the most concrete, tangible, and verifiable expressions of mutual recognition in this new phase of bilateral relations."

But an uneasy tension festered behind the fanfare.

"What many Venezuelans here in Miami-Dade truly want — what they dream of — is the day they can fly to a free Venezuela," Miami-Dade Mayor Daniella Levine Cava said, "where democracy is restored, the will of the people is respected [and] institutions are strengthened."

The newfound ties between the Trump administration and Rodriguez's government don't sit well with many Venezuelans, who in a poll this year overwhelmingly supported holding elections within a year. While Maduro is gone, the regime he led remains, even though Rodriguez has dropped the anti-American rhetoric and has publicly displayed an openness to working with U.S. officials.

At Miami's airport, Venezuelan journalists in exile pressed Plasencia about the possibility of returning to Venezuela and the more than 450 political prisoners still held here.

"I think we should focus on the new and leave behind negative rhetoric, the malice, and the effort to deceive — to keep deceiving Venezuelans and the world," he said. Plasencia said, "Venezuelans should be happy to have these connections," referring to the flights.

The directive to reopen the commercial flights came directly from Trump after the operation that snatched Maduro, who is in New York City facing drug-trafficking charges that he denies. In a Jan. 28 cabinet meeting, Trump instructed Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy and U.S. military officials to reopen commercial airspace over Venezuela by the end of the day.

The turnaround was unusually fast for an international market that had been closed for so long to U.S. carriers. While it usually would have taken six months to reopen such a route, American Airlines worked with U.S. and Venezuelan officials to do so in half the time, said José Freig, vice president of international operations for the airline. "This comeback is a record," he said.

Starting in mid-May, American Airlines will increase its service to two daily flights between Miami and Caracas.

Iris Suárez was pleased that she could travel to Miami on the same flight that had just arrived. "Good things are happening," she said. "The flight was expensive but I prefer to pay for a direct flight that helps me avoid problems with connections and having to travel eight to 10 hours to get to the U.S."

During the three-hour flight Thursday to Caracas, U.S. and Venezuelan officials chatted in the aisles with businessmen, journalists and a handful of ordinary passengers, who nibbled on plantain chips.

While officials cast the resumption of flights as a way to reunite families, it won't eliminate the barriers that still make it difficult or risky for many Venezuelans in the U.S. to travel home. Many Venezuelans and dual nationals face expired passports that are difficult to renew after years of disrupted Venezuelan consular services in the U.S.

Levine Cava, the Miami-Dade mayor, said her Venezuelan constituents — Miami has the largest Venezuelan exile community in the U.S. — fear returning to a country where the same repressive regime remains in power.

"We are very, very anxious to see real progress towards democratization, " she told The Wall Street Journal. "We're happy that something is moving forward, but at this point, we don't see that the voices of Venezuelans are reflected in the selection of the leadership. We don't see exactly the path towards moving in that direction, and that is what this community here is seeking."

Many Venezuelans are living in the U.S. under temporary or contested immigration protections, making it risky to leave the country. And for many Venezuelans looking to travel in the other direction, Trump's travel ban still partially suspends visas for the country's citizens.

U.S. citizens also continue to face serious travel warnings. While the State Department lowered its Venezuela advisory in March to Level 3 — Reconsider Travel — it still warns of crime, kidnapping, terrorism, and poor health infrastructure while keeping several parts of the country under "Do Not Travel" guidance.

Write to
